title: Nigeria's trial deficit: comparison with large developing countries
primary_estimand: Nigeria gap ratio: comparator per-capita trial rate divided by Nigeria per-capita rate
certainty: high (registry data, complete enumeration within ClinicalTrials.gov)
body: Why does Nigeria, Africa's most populous country and largest economy, register so few clinical trials relative to other large developing nations with comparable income levels? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for all interventional trials located in Nigeria and in six comparator countries, then calculated per-capita trial rates and pairwise gap ratios for each. Nigeria had 354 registered interventional trials for a population of 230 million people, yielding only 1.5 trials per million, which fell below India, Indonesia, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and every Latin American comparator in this sample. Brazil's per-capita rate was approximately thirty times higher than Nigeria's, and Bangladesh's was roughly two and a half times higher despite a lower national GDP per capita. Sickle cell disease trials were notably scarce despite Nigeria carrying the world's largest national burden of the condition. Trial activity was also heavily concentrated in Lagos, with much of northern Nigeria showing virtually no visible registry activity at all. The analysis is limited to ClinicalTrials.gov and cannot capture studies registered only on African regional or WHO trial platforms.
ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.

checks.value: 1.5 trials per million, worst among large developing countries
~150K SCD births/year yet scarce SCD trials
Lagos dominance, northern Nigeria absent from research
single registry, cannot capture African/WHO platform registrations
